coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly PEK-C-based membranes with high forward osmosis performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institution of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Yan Hua
Chen, Jian Hua
Yang, Qian
Zhuo, Yi Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Forward osmosis (FO), as an emerging energy-saving membrane processes, is highly promising to solve the water-shortage problem. Herein, a series of novel carbo polyetherketone (PEK-C)-based FO membranes are prepared via layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly by using polyethyleneimine (PEI) as the positive layer and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the negative layer. By tuning the number of self-assembly layers, the characteristics of outermost electrolyte, and the concentration of CQDs solution used for self-assembly, the performance of the PEK-C-based FO membranes can be optimized. The resulting membrane, [PEI/CQDs]2PEI, exhibits best performance with a water flux of 30.8 LMH and a reverse salt flux of 8.9 GMH, respectively, outperforming most of current FO membranes. Such high performance is attributed to the presence of CQDs, which endows the membrane with more hydrophilic surface. Furthermore, the membrane presents excellent long-term stability and anti-fouling property owing to the smoother surface that results from the LBL method. Our work could provide some reference for the design of high performance membrane.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Forward osmosis
Carbon quantum dots
Carbo polyetherketone
Layer-by-layer self-assembly reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs and FO membrane
Effects of the number of self-assembled layers and the characteristics of the outermost electrolyte on the PEK-C-based FO performance
Effects of the concentration of CQDs during self-assembled on the PEK-C-based FO performance
Comparison of FO performance of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI with other FO membrane
Performance of long-term stability and anti-fouling property of PEK-C FO membrane and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of the [PEI/CQDs]n/PEI FO membrane.
FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, PEI and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI.
XPS of CQDs: (A) XPS full scan spectrum, (B) N1s spectrum, (C) C1s spectrum, (D) O1s spectrum ; and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI: (E) XPS full scan spectrum; (F) C 1s spectrum, (G) N 1s spectrum,(H) O1s spectrum.
Zeta potential of the CQDs, PEI, [PEI/CQDs]2 and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI.
Contact angles of the FO membrane.
SEM images of FO membrane: (A) PEK-C, (B) [PEI/CQDs]1PEI, (C) [PEI/CQDs]2PEI, (D) [PEI/CQDs]3PEI, (E) [PEI/CQDs]2, and (F) cross-sectional morphology of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI.
AFM images of FO membranes: (A) PEK-C; (B) [PEI/CQDs]1PEI; (C) [PEI/CQDs]2PEI; (D) [PEI/CQDs]3PEI; (E) [PEI/CQDs]2.
Effect of the number of self-assembled layers and the characteristics of the outermost electrolyte on the PEK-C-based FO performance.
Effects of the concentration of CQDs during self-assembled on the PEK-C-based FO performance.
FO anti-fouling performance and long-term stability test.
Comparison of FO performance of some of FO membrane recently reported.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly PEK-C-based membranes with high forward osmosis performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Province University Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Province University Key Laboratory of Modern Analytical Science and Separation Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: NTNU Nanomechanical Lab, Department of Structural Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU), Trondheim 7491, Norway

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Environment, Minnan Normal University, Zhangzhou 363000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Forward osmosis (FO), as an emerging energy-saving membrane processes, is highly promising to solve the water-shortage problem. Herein, a series of novel carbo polyetherketone (PEK-C)-based FO membranes are prepared via layer-by-layer (LBL) self-assembly by using polyethyleneimine (PEI) as the positive layer and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as the negative layer. By tuning the number of self-assembly layers, the characteristics of outermost electrolyte, and the concentration of CQDs solution used for self-assembly, the performance of the PEK-C-based FO membranes can be optimized. The resulting membrane, [PEI/CQDs]2PEI, exhibits best performance with a water flux of 30.8 LMH and a reverse salt flux of 8.9 GMH, respectively, outperforming most of current FO membranes. Such high performance is attributed to the presence of CQDs, which endows the membrane with more hydrophilic surface. Furthermore, the membrane presents excellent long-term stability and anti-fouling property owing to the smoother surface that results from the LBL method. Our work could provide some reference for the design of high performance membrane.

ce:section.ce:para: With the world economy booming and the acceleration of the industrialization process, environmental problems have become increasingly prominent, and the shortage of water resources is particularly obvious (Wang and Yang, 2016). The total amount of water resources on the earth amounts is about 140,000m3, while freshwater resources account for only 2.5% of the total. According to reports, more than 1.2 billion people worldwide are unable to drink safe and hygienic fresh water, and more than 2.3 billion people are living in a water-deprived life (Zhao et al., 2012). Therefore, it is urgent to solve the shortage of water. To produce fresh water, membrane separation technologies have been widely used in seawater desalination (Kang et al., 2019a,b; Goh et al., 2019), industrial wastewater treatment (Pronk et al., 2019; Tanudjaja et al., 2019), food industry (Xu et al., 2019; Guimarães et al., 2019) due to their advantages of high energy efficiency, simple operation, and no secondary pollution. The current common membrane separation technologies can be divided into pressure-driven membrane separation technology and the spontaneous infiltration technology. The pressure-driven membrane separation technologies, such as microfiltration, ultrafiltration, reverse osmosis, nanofiltration, pervaporation, membrane distillation, etc., (Ng et al., 2021) usually face the problems of high energy consumption, demanding pretreatment processes, and irreversible membrane fouling (Badruzzaman et al., 2019; Jamaly et al., 2014). One of the examples of the spontaneous infiltration process is forward osmosis (FO), which has aroused enormous concern and developed rapidly due to its low energy consumption, less liable to the contamination, and high recovery rate (Li et al., 2019; Kim et al., 2019; Bao et al., 2019a,b; Kang et al., 2018). FO not only has the potential to solve the water-shortage problem but also can generate electricity, control drug release and food processing (Ang et al., 2019; Lutchmiah et al., 2014).

Interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction is a versatile and facile method for preparing membranes (Yip et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2019; Rastgar et al., 2018; Jeon et al., 2018). The membranes that prepared by IP reaction, for instance reacting polyamines (commonly used m-phenylenediamine (MPD)) with polyacid chloride (commonly used trimesoyl chloride (TMC)), often exhibit thin thickness, excellent FO water permeability and salt rejection performance. Nevertheless, the roughness of the synthesized active layer is relatively large, leading to the deposition of pollutants thus affecting the water flux and membrane antifouling characteristic. To enhance the fouling resistance, great efforts have been conducted to modify the polyamide active layer (Castrillón et al., 2014; Lu et al., 2013; Shaffer et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2016; Han et al., 2012). Among them, layer by layer (LBL) assembly, which uses charge interaction to deposit two types of materials with positive and negative charges on the membrane surface to form an active layer or modify the active layer, shows great potential (Qiu et al., 2011; Liu et al., 2013). By LBL, the surface charge and roughness of the membrane can be easily adjusted, thereby improving the separation properties of membrane and anti-pollution ability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can also be called carbon dots, which is an emerging carbon nanomaterial. CQDs are usually spherical nanoparticles and amorphous nanocrystalline nuclei with diameters between 3 and 20nm (Mandani et al., 2017; Baker and Baker, 2010; Sahu et al., 2012). The surface of CQDs contains many epoxy groups, hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups. According to the different synthetic routes, the oxygen contents range from 5% to 50% (Sahu et al., 2012). Incorporating CQDs into membranes, such as nanofiltration membrane (Shen et al., 2012), reverse osmosis membrane (Bi et al., 2018), and ultrafiltration membrane (Song et al., 2015; Zeng et al., 2016), has demonstrated the ability to improve both water flux and anti-fouling property. Unlike tubular and sheet nano materials, CQDs are one kind of zero-dimensional (0 D) nanomaterial, which can easily wrap in a polymer matrix network to form a defect-free selective layer. In addition, CQDs can distribute in the membrane more uniformly, resulting a smoother surface, which benefits the anti-fouling performance. It can be expected that CQDs-incorporated FO membrane may achieve excellent separation performances and anti-fouling properties simultaneously.

LBL assembly is a promising strategy for fabricating high-performance FO membranes with a controlled and ultrathin selective layer (Ralph et al., 2018; Soon-Bum et al., 2015; Wafa et al., 2019). Poly-ethyleneimine (PEI) is a commercially available polymer that contains plenty of primary, secondary, and tertiary amine groups, soluble in water and owing of high cationic charge-density (Ahmad et al., 2021). Herein, we use PEI as the cation material and CQDs as the anion material to modify the polyamide layer of the surface of PEK-C FO membrane via LBL to improve the separation performance and the ability of anti-fouling of the PEK-C-based FO membrane. The presence of CQDs grants a flawless selective layer, thus enhancing the separation properties. By tuning the number of self-assembled layers, the characteristics of the outermost electrolyte and the concentration of CQDs used for self-assembly modification, we prepare a series of membranes with excellent performance.

Carbo polyetherketone (PEK-C) was obtained from Xuzhou Engineering Plastic Factory. Polyethylene pyrrolidone (PVP) was purchased from Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), m-Phenylenediamine (MPD), tartaric acid, trimesoyl chloride (TMC), and polyethyleneimine (PEI) were all acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Magnesium chloride (MgCl2∙6H2O) and n-Hexane were acquired from Shanghai Xilong Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Milk was obtained from Fujian Changfu Dairy Products Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received.

25ml of milk was taken into a 100ml Teflon-lined autoclave and heated in an oil bath at 180°C for 2h. Then the dark brown liquid was obtained after cooling at room temperature. Larger dots were removed by centrifugation of liquid at 10000rpm for 10min. Finally, the liquid was dialyzed for 48h and was freeze–dried in a lyophilizer for 48h to obtain CQDs.

The casting solution for preparing of PEK-C porous supporting layer composed of 14wt% PEK-C, 3wt% PVP and 83wt% NMP was prepared by stirring the mixtures at room temperature for about 2h until a homogeneous solution was obtained. After standing in air to remove bubbles naturally, the casting solution was cast on a clean and dry glass plate with a casting knife of 150μm thickness, and then it was immersed rapidly in deionized water of 40°C. The prepared PEK-C porous supporting layers were kept in deionized water of 25°C for 24h to wash completely out the solvents.

The prepared PEK-C porous supporting layer was submerged in 2.0 %(w/v) MPD aqueous solution for 2min and the excess MPD solution was drained away from the surface of porous supporting layers. Then 0.15% (w/v) TMC solution in hexane was poured onto the top surface of porous supporting layers, and the excess TMC solution was drained away from the surface of porous supporting layers after 3.5min contact time. Finally, it was dried at 25°C for 30min, and the PEK-C FO membrane was obtained.

Using PEI as the positive layer and CQDs as the negative layer, top surface of the PEK-C FO membrane was initially put in contact with 1g/L PEI solutions for 10min and then with CQDs solution for 10min. After each drenched step, the PEK-C FO membrane was washed with deionized water for 5min to remove excess polyelectrolytes. The preparation process of the [PEI/CQDs]n/PEI FO membrane is shown in the Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and FO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and dimension of CQDs was obtained by transmission electron microscope (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 120kV (Tecnai G2 20, FEI Company). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was examined using Nicolet iS10 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.) with the spectral range from 4000 to 400cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurements were carried out by Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.).

The membrane morphologies were observed via a scanning electronic microscope (SEM) (JEOL, S-4800, Japan Hitachi Company) and an atomic force microscope (AFM) (CSPM5500, China Benyuan Nano Instruments Co., Ltd). In additions, the membranes were fractured in liquid nitrogen before sputtering with platinum, then the cross profiles of the membranes were tested by SEM. The contact angles of the membranes were tested with a contact angle meter (SL200B, Solon Tech, Shanghai, China).

The performances (water flux and reverse salt flux) of FO membrane were tested using a lab-scale cross-flow FO setup at room temperature. If there is no special note, both feed solution (DS) and draw solution (DS) were circulated at a flow rate of 200ml/min using DI water as FS and 2.0M MgCl2 solution as DS. The reverse salt flux of the DS was determined used a conductivity meter (DDS-11A, Rex, China). The mode of FO experiments chose the AL-FS orientation, which the active layer is in contact with the FS. To reach the steady state, the FO setup was run for 30min before the test. The curve of water flux was drawn simultaneously by recording the mass of the FS tank. Each test was repeated at least three times, and then the average value was calculated.

The FO water flux Jw (L/m2 h, LMH) and reverse salt flux Js (g/m2 h, GMH) was determined by the following Eq. (1) and (2), respectively.
(1)
J w
=
Δ m
t × S × ρ
(2)
J s
=
Δ w
t × S
where Δm (g) is the mass variations of DI water, S (m2) is the effective membrane area, t (h) is the time interval of testing, ρ (g/m3) is the density of water, ΔW (g) is mass variations of MgCl2 in the FS, respectively.

The anti-fouling testing of the FO membranes was performed under AL-FS at 25°C. BSA aqueous solution of 100mg/L was selected as the FS, while 2.0M MgCl2 was utilized as the DS. The FO setup was running for 2.5h to conduct the anti-fouling testing. After testing, the surface of membranes was washed with DI water for 30min to remove the pollutant. The steps were repeated for three times.

CQDs is prepared by hydro-thermal treatment with pure milk based on previous report (Wang et al., 2014). The TEM images of CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . It indicates that CQDs present good spherical structure and relatively uniform size. The functional groups contained on the surface of CQDs dominate their water solubility, which can affect the LBL process and quality of prepared membranes. Herein, the functional groups of CQDs are investigated by FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 3 , the CQDs have a strong absorption peak around 3433cm−1, which is a typical OH stretching vibration region, indicating that the CQDs have rich hydroxyl groups and good water solubility. The absorption peak at 1652cm−1 is CO stretching vibration, and the absorption peak at 1542cm−1 corresponds to NH stretching vibration. A large number of hydrophilic groups endow the prepared CQDs with strong hydrophilicity, facilitating the fabrication of ultrathin selective layer of membrane by LBL. As for the PEI, the peak around 3562cm−1 belongs to OH and NH stretching vibrations and the peak around 3070cm−1 attributes to NH stretching vibration. The prominent peak observed at 1661cm−1 is due to NH bending. Furthermore, the small band at 1597cm cm−1 is assigned to amine groups (Ahmad et al., 2021). In the FT-IR spectrum of the [PEI/CQDs]2PEI membrane, there are two characteristic absorbance bands located at 1647cm−1 and 1583cm−1, which correspond to the CO stretching vibration of NHCO and the NH bending of NH2 (Liu et al., 2013), respectively. Meanwhile, the peak around 3514cm−1 and 3056cm-1 belongs to OH and NH stretching vibrations all shifts to lower frequency. The FT-IR results prove that CQDs and PEI had been successfully assembled on the surface of the PEK-C FO membrane.

To further determine the functional groups and atomic content of CQDs, XPS is conducted. As shown in Fig. 4 (A), CQDs is consisted of C, N, and O with atomic percentages of 63.1%, 11.5%, and 25.4%, respectively. The binding energies in N1s spectrum of 399.7 and 400.9eV are corresponding to CN and NH or CN, respectively (Fig. 4(B)). The narrow-scan spectrum of the C1s shows that CC/C-C, CO/C-N, and OC=O bonds exist in the CQDs, with the binding energies of 284.4, 286.9, and 288.1eV, respectively (Fig. 4(C)). The main peaks of the O1s resolution spectrum (Fig. 4(D)) at 531.4 and 532.8eV correspond to the CO and COH/COC groups of CQDs, respectively. It is noted that the content of CO and COH/COC groups was essential to the good hydrophilic of the CQDs. Apart from O atoms, N atom also plays important role in enhancing the hydrophilic of the membrane. The XPS characterization data is consistent with FI-IR data, demonstrating that CQDs contains plenty of hydrophilic groups. The XPS of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI is shown in Fig. 4E, The B.E. plots show three prominent peaks corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s for [PEI/CQDs]2PEI. The N1s peak of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI (Fig. 4F) clearly showed the existence of a CN peak at 399.4eV, whereas for CQDs the CN peak was at 399.7eV. Additionally, the B.E. plots of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI (Fig. 4G) exhibit a weakened CO peak while CQDs show a prominent peak corresponding to CO at 288.1eV. The deconvoluted O1s of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI (Fig. 4H) exhibits a clear COH peak at 530.9eV, there is almost no change compared with CQDs. All of the results indicate that the PEI and CQDs are successfully introduced as the positive and negative layers through electrostatic force or intermolecular forces. Thus the layer-by-layer self-assembly of the membrane can be realized by using PEI and CQDs.

The evaluation of the charge property of CQDs, PEI, [PEI/CQDs]2 and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI are measurement by a Zeta potential analyzer. As shown in Fig. 5 , it is found that the CQDs is negatively charged due to the plenty of hydroxyl groups and carboxyl groups, as confirmed by FI-TR and XPS analysis. It also can be seen from Fig. 5 that PEI is positively charged, and the characteristics of the outermost layer of self-assembled material will affect the zeta potential of the membrane.

As schematically shown in Fig. 1, the FO membrane is prepared by LBL. In this process, after deposition of the positively charged PEI, the top of the PEI is endowed with negatively charged CQDs, thus constituting a bilayer membrane. With the hydrophilicity of the membranes improved, the membranes are beneficial to improve water flux and anti-fouling performance. The water contact angles of the surface of the membranes are the main basis for characterizing the hydrophilicity of the membrane. As shown in Fig. 6 , as the number of self-assembled layers increases, the water contact angles show a downward trend. Because the self-assembly bring the surface of PEK-C-based FO membranes with a large number of amino or carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups, improving the hydrophilicity of the PEK-C-based FO membrane. And the membrane with the outermost layer of PEI is more hydrophilic than that of the membrane with the outermost layer of CQDs. This is attributed to a large number of amino groups on the PEI chain, which increase the hydrophilicity of the membrane.

The morphology of membranes affects the anti-fouling performance. The smoother surface of the membrane can build up the property of anti-fouling which can weaken the deposition of pollutant. The surface SEM images of the prepared membranes are shown in Fig.7 . The PEK-C-based FO membrane present undulations and rough surface, which is morphology characteristic of the polyamide peaks and ridges (Fig. 7(A)). As the number of self-assembled layers increases, the surface of the PEK-C-based FO membrane becomes denser and the characteristic peaks of the polyamide valley ridges become smaller. In comparison with PEK-C FO membrane, the surface morphology of [PEI/CQDs]3PEI FO membrane (Fig. 7(D)) is more smoother and denser. As the number of self-assembled layers increases, the concentration of PEI and CQDs on the surface of the PEK-C-based FO membrane increases, and the molecular chains of the electrolyte stretches and deposits into a dense layer. From the comparison of surface morphology between [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane (Fig. 7(C)) and [PEI/CQDs]2 FO membrane (Fig. 7(E)), whether the outermost layer with PEI or CQDs, the surface roughness of membranes are obviously similar. It indicates that the number of self-assembled layers has a major effect on the roughness of surface. From Fig. 7(F), we can find that the thickness of the self-assembled dense layer is about 1.8μm.

We also use AFM to further study the morphology of the membranes. As shown in Fig. 8 , the obtained arithmetic average roughness (Ra) of PEK-C, [PEI/CQDs]1PEI, [PEI/CQDs]2PEI, [PEI/CQDs]3PEI and [PEI/CQDs]2 membranes are 136nm, 88nm, 78nm, 47nm, and 76nm, respectively. As the number of self-assembled layers increases, the average roughness of the surface of PEK-C-based FO membrane continues to decrease, and gradually becomes smoother and flatter, which is consistent with the results of SEM characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the number of self-assembled layers and the characteristics of the outermost electrolyte on the PEK-C-based FO performance
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the membrane, we study the effects of the number of self-assembled layers and the characteristics of the outermost electrolyte on the PEK-C-based FO performance. As the number of assembly layers increases, the water flux of the [PEI/CQDs]nPEI FO membrane increases firstly and then decreases, while the [PEI/CQDs]n FO membrane shows a slight decrease, as shown in Fig. 9 . With the increase in the number of assembly layers, the reverse salt flux of [PEI/CQDs]n decrease less, but the reverse salt flux of [PEI/CQDs]nPEI decrease greatly. This can be attributed to the increased membrane thickness due to the increase of the number of assembled layers, which increases the diffusion resistance of Mg2+ ions. At the same time, the positively charge PEI in the outermost layer of the membrane also repels Mg2+ ions.

The characteristics of the outermost electrolyte of the PEK-C-based FO membrane have a great influence on its FO performance. When the outermost electrolyte of the PEK-C-based FO membrane is PEI, its water flux is higher than that of CQDs, while the reverse salt flux shows an opposite trend. Because PEI is the positively charged polyelectrolyte which blocks Mg2+ ions out of the membrane through electrostatic action, thus enhancing the Mg2+ ions rejective effect. Based on the results of contact angle test, the membranes with the outermost layer of PEI exhibit higher hydrophilicity, which is in favor of the water flux.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of the concentration of CQDs during self-assembled on the PEK-C-based FO performance
ce:section.ce:para: To study the effect of concentration of CQDs on the PEK-C-based FO membrane performance, we tune the concentration of CQDs during preparation of FO membranes. As shown in Fig. 10 , with the increase of CQDs concentration, the water flux of the PEK-C-based FO membrane decreases slightly, while the reverse salt flux decreases sharply. By increasing the concentration of CQDs from 0.5g/L to 1.0g/L, the water flux shows 7.2% of decline, while the reverse salt flux presents 51.4% of reduction. With the increase of CQDs concentration, there are more hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of PEK-C-based FO membrane surface, so more positively charged PEI can be self-assembled. Therefore, it can promote the increase of water flux, but is adverse to the diffusion of Mg2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of FO performance of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI with other FO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the separation performance of our [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane, we compare it with some of other FO membranes recently reported, as shown in Table 1 . We can find that the performance of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane is better than that of other membranes when MgCl2 is used as the draw solute; however, the performance of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane is in general when NaCl is used as the draw solute. This maybe attributes the fact that under the same concentration, MgCl2 has higher osmotic pressure than that of NaCl.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of long-term stability and anti-fouling property of PEK-C FO membrane and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: The long-term stability and anti-fouling property of the FO membranes are of great importance for practical application. Therefore, we investigate the long-term stability and anti-fouling property of PEK-C FO membrane and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane by feeding DI water and BSA solution, respectively. As shown in Fig. 11 , when the feed solution is DI water, after 2.5h testing, the water flux of membranes both slightly decrease. [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane exhibits higher stability in comparison with PEK-C FO membrane. Comparing with PEK-C FO membrane, [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane shows lower decline in water flux during the feeding of 100mg/L BSA solution, demonstrating good anti-fouling performance. After 2.5h testing, the water flux of PEK-C FO membrane and [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane show 15.7% and 10.1% of decline, respectively. Since self-assembly makes the roughness of the active layer of the membrane smaller than that of the PEK-C FO membrane and improves the hydrophilicity of the membrane, [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane has stronger anti-fouling ability than PEK-C FO membrane. After washing with DI water for 30min, the water flux of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane nearly returned to the original value.

In this study, PEI/CQDs-modified novel PEK-C-based FO membranes were fabricated via layer-by-layer self-assembly technology to modify the surface of PEK-C FO membrane. The prepared PEK-C-based FO membranes were characterized by various methods, and their performance of were also investigated. The characteristics of the outermost electrolyte of the PEK-C-based FO membrane have a remarkable influence on its separation performance. When the outermost electrolyte of the PEK-C-based FO membrane is PEI, the water flux is higher than that of the membrane that CQDs as outermost later, and its reverse salt flux is just the opposite. With the increase of self-assembled layer, the water flux of the PEK-C-based FO membrane tends to rise first and then decrease, and the reverse salt flux shows a downward trend. With the increase of CQDs concentration, the water flux of the PEK-C-based FO membrane decreases slightly, while the reverse salt flux decreases significantly. The water flux and the reverse salt flux of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membrane reach 30.8 LMH and 8.9 GMH, respectively. The performance of stability and anti-fouling of [PEI/CQDs]2PEI FO membranes are inspiring.
There are no conflicts to declare.

Yan Hua Deng: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Validation. Jian Hua Chen: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Validation. Qian Yang: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Software. Yi Zhi Zhuo: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Data curation, Resources.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21676133) and Natural Science Foundation of Fujian (No. 2014J01051). The author sincerely thanks the editor-in-chief and anonymous reviewer for their help in this work.
